statements.sql: UPDATE tgt SET tgt.EnglishProductName = src.ProductName, tgt.LastSeenDate = GETDATE() FROM silver.UpdateJoinTarget AS tgt INNER JOIN bronze.Product AS src ON tgt.ProductAlternateKey = CAST(src.ProductID AS NVARCHAR(25))

refactor.extracted_sql: SELECT tgt.ProductAlternateKey, src.ProductName AS EnglishProductName, GETDATE() AS LastSeenDate FROM silver.UpdateJoinTarget AS tgt INNER JOIN bronze.Product AS src ON tgt.ProductAlternateKey = CAST(src.ProductID AS NVARCHAR(25))
refactor.refactored_sql: WITH import AS ( SELECT tgt.ProductAlternateKey, src.ProductName, src.ProductID FROM silver.UpdateJoinTarget AS tgt INNER JOIN bronze.Product AS src ON tgt.ProductAlternateKey = CAST(src.ProductID AS NVARCHAR(25)) ), logical AS ( SELECT ProductAlternateKey, ProductName AS EnglishProductName, GETDATE() AS LastSeenDate FROM import ), final AS ( SELECT ProductAlternateKey, EnglishProductName, LastSeenDate FROM logical ) SELECT * FROM final

source_tables.summary: Both extracted and refactored SQL join the same source tables: silver.UpdateJoinTarget and bronze.Product.

output_columns.summary: Both queries return the same three columns: ProductAlternateKey, EnglishProductName (mapped from ProductName), and LastSeenDate (computed from GETDATE()).

joins.summary: Both use identical INNER JOIN logic on ProductAlternateKey = CAST(src.ProductID AS NVARCHAR(25)). Refactored SQL preserves this join condition in the import CTE.

filters.summary: Neither query applies filtering; both process all matched rows from the INNER JOIN.

aggregation_grain.summary: Both queries operate at row level with no aggregation. Each matched source row produces one output row.
